volumeTitle: Persians. Seven against Thebes. Suppliants. Prometheus Bound
label: Persians (Section 1 - Section 230)

sections.label: Section 1
sections.translation: Here we are , the faithful Council of the Persians , who have gone to the land of Hellas , we who serve as warders of the royal abode , rich in bountiful store of gold ,

sections.label: Section 5
sections.translation:
we whom Xerxes , our King , Darius ’ royal son , himself selected , by virtue of our rank and years , to be the guardians of his realm .
Yet as regards the return of our King and of his host , so richly decked out in gold ,

sections.label: Section 10
sections.translation: the soul within my breast is distressed and presages disaster . For the whole populace of the Asian nation has come and murmurs against its youthful King , nor does any courier or horseman

sections.label: Section 15
sections.translation: arrive at the city of the Persians , who left behind them the walled defence of Susa and Agbatana and Cissa ’ s ancient ramparts , and went forth , some on horseback , some in galleys , others on foot

sections.label: Section 20
sections.translation:
presenting a dense array of war .
Such are Amistres and Artaphrenes and Megabates and Astaspes , marshals of the Persians ; kings themselves , yet vassals of the Great King ,

sections.label: Section 25
sections.translation: they press on , commanders of an enormous host , skilled in archery and horsemanship , formidable to look upon and fearful in battle through the valiant resolve of their souls . Artembares , too , who fights from his chariot ,

sections.label: Section 30
sections.translation: and Masistres , and noble Imaeus , skilled with the bow , and Pharandaces , and Sosthanes , who urges on his steeds . Others in addition the mighty , fecund Nile sent forth — Susiscanes ,

sections.label: Section 35
sections.translation: Pegastagon of Egyptian lineage , mighty Arsames , lord of sacred Memphis , Ariomardus , governor of ancient Thebes , and the marsh-dwelling oarsmen ,

sections.label: Section 40
sections.translation: well-skilled and countless in number .

sections.label: Section 41
sections.translation: Behind them follows a throng of luxurious Lydians and those [*] who hold in subjection all the people of the mainland , whom Metrogathes and brave Arcteus , their regal commanders ,

sections.label: Section 45
sections.translation: and Sardis rich in gold sent forth , riding in many a chariot , in ranks with three and four steeds abreast , a spectacle terrible to behold . They too who live by sacred Tmolus pledge themselves

sections.label: Section 50
sections.translation: to cast the yoke of slavery upon Hellas — Mardon , Tharybis , anvils of the lance , and the Mysians , hurlers of the javelin . Babylon , also , teeming with gold , sends a mixed host arrayed in a long line , both mariners borne in galleys

sections.label: Section 55
sections.translation:
and those who rely on their skill in archery . The nation too which wears the sabre follows from every part of Asia in the fearful procession of the King .
Such are the warriors , the flower of the Persian land ,

sections.label: Section 60
sections.translation: who have departed , and in fierce longing for them the whole land of Asia , their foster-nurse , laments , while parents and wives , as they count the days , shudder at the lengthening delay .

sections.label: Section 1.65
sections.translation: The royal army , dealing destruction to cities , has already passed to the neighboring land upon the facing shore , and this they did by crossing the Hellespont ,

sections.label: Section 1.70
sections.translation: named for the daughter of Athamas , on a bridge of boats made fast with cables , thereby casting a tightly constructed roadway as a yoke upon the neck of the sea .

sections.label: Section 1.73
sections.translation: The fiery lord of populous Asia

sections.label: Section 1.75
sections.translation: is leading his wondrous warrior-flock against the whole earth in two divisions , on foot and by the sea , putting his trust in his stalwart and stern commanders ; he himself ,

sections.label: Section 1.80
sections.translation: a god-like hero whose race is sprung from gold . [*]

sections.label: Section 2.81
sections.translation: With eyes flashing with the dark glare of a deadly dragon , attended by soldiers and mariners in great numbers , and speeding his Syrian chariot ,

sections.label: Section 2.85
sections.translation: he leads against a people renowned for the spear a warlike host of archers .

sections.label: Section 2.87
sections.translation: And there is no man skilled to withstand the mighty stream of men , and with strong barriers keep out the sea ’ s invincible surge ;

sections.label: Section 2.90
sections.translation: for Persia ’ s host cannot be withstood , and her men are courageous .

sections.label: Section 3.93
sections.translation: For by the will of the gods Fate has held sway

sections.label: Section 3.95
sections.translation: since ancient time , and has ordained for the Persians the pursuit of rampart-destroying war , the turmoil of fighting horsemen , and the storming of cities .

sections.label: Section 4.100
sections.translation: And they have learned to look upon the precinct of the deep when the broad-wayed sea whitens to foam beneath the tempest ’ s blast , trusting in their finely wrought

sections.label: Section 4.105
sections.translation: cables and their devices which give passage to their army .

sections.label: Section 4.107
sections.translation: Yet the insidious guile of god — what mortal man can escape it ? Who with agile foot can lightly overleap

sections.label: Section 3.110
sections.translation: and escape its toils ?

sections.label: Section 3.111
sections.translation: For Delusion , with her fair appearance , lures men astray into her snares , from which it is not possible to escape without meeting with disaster .

sections.label: Section 5.115
sections.translation: Therefore my heart is wrapped in gloom and is racked with fear for the Persian army lest the state learn that the mighty capital of Susa is empty of men .

sections.label: Section 5.120
sections.translation: Fearful I am too that as bands of women cry aloud woe , the Cissian stronghold will raise a resounding shout responsive to the beating of breasts ,

sections.label: Section 5.125
sections.original: βυσσίνοις δ ἐν πέπλοις πέσῃ λακίς . [*]
sections.translation: and that there will be a tearing of fine linen robes .

sections.label: Section 6.126
sections.translation: For all the men-at-arms , those who urge on steeds and those who march along the plain , have left the city and gone forth , like bees in a swarm , together with the captain of the host .

sections.label: Section 6.130
sections.translation: They have crossed the headland , projecting into the sea and common to either continent , through which both shores are bridged .

sections.label: Section 6.133
sections.translation: And marriage-beds are filled with tears through longing for husbands ;

sections.label: Section 6.135
sections.translation: each Persian woman has sent to the field her warlike and fiery consort , and now in grief and longing for her beloved lord , is left forsaken by her mate .

sections.label: Section 140
sections.translation: But come , Persians , let us take our place on the steps of this ancient palace and devise some wise and deeply-pondered counsel — for need of this has come upon us — as to how Xerxes our King ,

sections.label: Section 145
sections.translation: Darius ’ son , scion of our own race as his forefather ’ s name declares , is faring . Is it the drawing of the bow that has triumphed , or is it the might of the sharp spear-head which has prevailed ? Enter Atossa , richly dressed , on a chariot and attended by a numerous retinue

sections.label: Section 150
sections.translation: But look , here is a light like the eyes of the god , the mother of our king , my Queen . I bow low before her . It is fitting also that we all address her with words of salutation . The elders prostrate themselves and then rise to their feet . Their leader continues

sections.label: Section 155
sections.translation: O Queen , most exalted of Persia ’ s deep-girdled women , venerable mother of Xerxes , wife of Darius , all hail ! You were the consort of the Persian ’ s god , and of another god the mother , that is , unless its former good fortune has now forsaken our host .

sections.label: Section 159
sections.original: ταῦτα δὴ λιποῦσ ἱκάνω χρυσεοστόλμους δόμους
sections.translation: For this very reason I have left the gold-decorated palace

sections.label: Section 160
sections.original: καὶ τὸ Δαρείου τε κἀμὸν κοινὸν εὐνατήριον .
sections.translation: and the chamber which belongs to Darius and myself , and have come here . My heart , too , is racked with anxiety , and to you , my friends , will I make a disclosure . For I am by no means free from apprehension that wealth , grown great , will , raising a cloud of dust upon the ground , trip up the prosperity which Darius raised not without the favor of some god .

sections.label: Section 165
sections.original: ταῦτά μοι διπλῆ μέριμνα φραστός [*] ἐστιν ἐν φρεσίν ,
sections.translation: It is for this reason that there is a double concern in my mind : neither to hold in honor vast wealth without men , and that the light of success does not shine , in proportion to their strength , on men without riches . Our wealth , at all events , is ample , but my anxiety is for the light , the salvation of the house , which I regard to be the presence of its lord .

sections.label: Section 170
sections.original: πρὸς τάδ ὡς οὕτως ἐχόντων τῶνδε , σύμβουλοι λόγου
sections.translation: Therefore , since things stand as they do , lend me your counsel in this concern , Persians , my aged trusty servants . For all my hopes of good counsel depend on you .

sections.label: Section 173
sections.original: εὖ τόδ ἴσθι , γῆς ἄνασσα τῆσδε , μή σε δὶς φράσαι
sections.translation: Be assured , our country ’ s Queen , that you need not twice mention either word or deed regarding that in which it is possible for us to direct you .

sections.label: Section 175
sections.original: εὐμενεῖς γὰρ ὄντας ἡμᾶς τῶνδε συμβούλους καλεῖς .
sections.translation: For we whom you summon as counsellors in these matters are well disposed towards you and your interests .

sections.label: Section 176
sections.translation: I have been haunted by a multitude of dreams at night since the time when my son , having despatched his army , departed with intent to lay waste the land of the Ionians . But never yet have I beheld so distinct a vision

sections.label: Section 180
sections.original: ὡς τῆς πάροιθεν εὐφρόνης : λέξω δέ σοι .
sections.translation:
as that of the last night . This I will describe to you .
I dreamed that two women in beautiful clothes , one in Persian garb , the other in Dorian attire , appeared before my eyes ; both far more striking in stature than are the women of our time ,

sections.label: Section 185
sections.original: κάλλει τ ἀμώμω , καὶ κασιγνήτα γένους
sections.translation: flawless in beauty , sisters of the same family . As for the lands in which they dwelt , to one had been assigned by lot the land of Hellas , to the other that of the barbarians . The two , as I imagined it , seemed to provoke each other to a mutual feud , and my son , when he had become aware of this ,

sections.label: Section 190
sections.translation: attempted to restrain and placate them . He yoked them both to his car and placed the collar-straps upon their necks . The one bore herself proudly in these trappings and kept her mouth obedient to the rein . The other struggled and with her hands

sections.label: Section 195
sections.translation: tore apart the harness of the car ; then , free of the curb , she dragged it violently along with her and snapped the yoke in two . My son was hurled to the ground and his father Darius stood by his side filled with pity . But Xerxes , when he caught sight of him , tore the garments covering his body .

sections.label: Section 200
sections.original: καὶ ταῦτα μὲν δὴ νυκτὸς εἰσιδεῖν λέγω .
sections.translation: Such was the vision I beheld in the night . But when I had risen and dipped my hands in the clear-flowing water of a spring , I drew near to an altar with incense in my hand , intending to make an offering of sacrificial cake to the divinities that avert evil , those to whom these rites are due .

sections.label: Section 205
sections.original: ὁρῶ δὲ φεύγοντ αἰετὸν πρὸς ἐσχάραν
sections.translation: But I saw an eagle fleeing for safety to the altar of Phoebus — and out of terror , my friends , I stood speechless . Thereupon I caught sight of a falcon rushing at full speed with outstretched wings and with his talons plucking at the head of the eagle , which did nothing but cower and

sections.label: Section 210
sections.translation:
yielded its body to his foe .
These are the terrors I beheld , terrors too for you to hear . Be assured that if my son should suceeed , he would prove to be remarkable indeed , but if he fails , he does not have to answer to the state ; if he returns safely , he will hold sway in this land as he did before .

sections.label: Section 215
sections.original: οὔ σε βουλόμεσθα , μῆτερ , οὔτ ἄγαν φοβεῖν λόγοις
sections.translation: Mother , we would neither alarm you unduly by our words nor raise your hopes too high . If , however , it is something inauspicious that you have seen , visit the gods with supplication and entreat them to avert the evil and to bring to pass what will be beneficial to you , your children , the kingdom , and all else that you hold dear . Next , it is appropriate that you should

sections.label: Section 220
sections.original: γῇ τε καὶ φθιτοῖς χέασθαι : πρευμενῶς δ αἰτοῦ τάδε ,
sections.translation: offer libations to Earth and the dead ; and use auspicious words to address your husband Darius , whom you say you have seen in the night , and ask him to send into the light of day from beneath the earth blessings for you and your son ; ask too that the reverse of this may be held in bondage beneath the earth and fade away in gloom . Such is the advice I , relying on my instincts , offer you with kind intent .

sections.label: Section 225
sections.original: εὖ δὲ πανταχῇ τελεῖν σοι τῶνδε κρίνομεν πέρι .
sections.translation: According to our interpretation of these portents , the issue will in all respects prove prosperous to you .

sections.label: Section 226
sections.original: ἀλλὰ μὴν εὔνους γ ὁ πρῶτος τῶνδ ἐνυπνίων κριτὴς
sections.translation: You , its first interpreter , have indeed read the meaning of my dream with goodwill , at least , toward my son and house . May the outcome then prove beneficial ! When I return to the palace , I will perform for the gods and my dear ones beneath the earth all those rites which you recommend . Meanwhile , my friends , I would like to learn

sections.label: Section 230
sections.original: εὖτ ἂν εἰς οἴκους μόλωμεν . κεῖνο [*] δ ἐκμαθεῖν θέλω ,
sections.translation: where Athens is located .
